title: practical english usage - Part 147
content:
After as, how, so, too and this/that meaning so, adjectives go before a/an.

187  adjectives       : position      a fter   as,   how,     so,   too Have you read anything interesting lately? Let's go somewhere quiet.

anywhere and similar words.

Adjectives come  after something, everything, anything, nothing, somebody, 6 something,           everything,           etc or (   the house next to the Royal Hotel) a difficult problem to solve the next house to the Royal Hotel     the best mother in the world a different life from this one       the second train from this platform comparatives and superlatives; and a few other adjectives like difficult and easy.

This happens with different, similar, the same, next, last, first, second, etc; In some cases an adjective can be put before a noun and its complement after it.

We are looking for people who are skilled in design.

A  relative clause is often more natural.

We are looking for people skilled in design, (     .. .skilled in design people.) expression normally comes after a noun.

When  an adjective has its own complement (e.g. skilled in design), the whole 5  adjectives        with    complements:         people        skilled       in   design Exception: worth (e.g. worth 100 euros). ►    634 two metres high ten years older two miles long  six feet deep Adjectives usually follow measurement nouns.

4 expressions         of  measurement:         two     metres        high For the position and meaning of opposite, ►    548.

After two days crossing the foothills, they reached the mountain proper.

Snowdon's a proper mountain, not a hill.

After a noun it refers to the central or main part of something. Compare: Before a noun, proper (especially in British English) means 'real', 'genuine'.

the members present (= those who  are/were at the meeting) the present members (= those who  are members now) absent'. Compare: Before a noun, present refers to time; after a noun it means 'here/there', 'not 3 present,        proper grammar *188  adjectives without nouns ... Stephen Gray and Susan Cook; the latter is a well-known designer.

The accused was released on bail.

the latter.

These include the accused, the undersigned, the deceased, the former and In a few formal fixed phrases, the + adjective can have a singular meaning.

3 singular      examples:      the    accused not                          or the Danes or the Turks (     the Danish   the Turkish).

Where nouns exist, these are preferred to expressions with the.. .ish: we  say not Irishwoman, a Welshman (     a-Welsk).

These expressions are plural; singular equivalents are for example an The Irish are very proud of their sense of humour.

Dutch, French.

without nouns. They  include Irish, Welsh, English, British, Spanish, A  few adjectives of nationality ending in -sh or -ch (►  321.3)  are used after the 2 adjectives        of  nationality:       the    Irish;   the    Dutch opportunities for both rich and poor both ... and ...

However, adjectives without the are sometimes used in paired structures with There are more unemployed than ever before.

This government doesn't care about the poor, (     ... about poor.) Adjectives are normally only used in this way with the or a determiner.

or                                                          not the problems of the poor   poor people's problems (     the poor's problems) Note that these expressions cannot be used with a possessive 's.

The dead have no further worries, (           The dead has-...) 'the dead people', but not 'the dead person'.

These expressions are normally plural: the dead means 'all dead people' or After the accident, the injured were taken to hospital.

The  meaning is usually general; occasionally a limited group is referred to.

The unemployed are losing hope.

He's collecting money for the blind.

disabilities generally prefer the adjective disabled.

The  term handicapped is now often considered offensive; people with physical the deaf     the jobless   the poor     the young the dead     the handicapped  the old      the unemployed the blind     the disabled    the mentally ill  the rich are in a particular physical or social condition. Common expressions: The + adjective is used to talk about certain well-known groups of people who 1  well-known        groups:     the    old;   the    poor But there are some exceptions.
